serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of packaged kefir is approximately 200 ml (200 g), equivalent to one small bottle or one cup as commonly sold in Singapore supermarkets.

nutrient_source.title: Kefir, lowfat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170904)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary macros (energy 83 kcal, protein 5.83 g, fat 3.33 g, carbohydrate 8.33 g, sugar 8.33 g, sodium 50 mg, dietary fibre 0 g) are sourced from the Singapore HPB Foodo database (cr_id D02020005, confidence 0.95). Secondary micronutrients (saturated fat, potassium, calcium, phosphorus, vitamins) are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for kefir (FDC ID 170904) as the closest approved global equivalent. Where HPB and USDA values are consistent, HPB values are retained as primary. Calcium reported as 0 mg in HPB source; USDA reports ~150 mg/100 g for comparable kefir — this discrepancy is noted and the HPB value is preserved per source-priority rules, with the USDA figure cited as a reference range in notes.

gi_source.title: Kefir, fermented milk — Glycemic Index Database, University of Sydney (Atkinson et al., 2008; Ostman et al., 2001)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for packaged sweetened kefir in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 36 is estimated from the closest approved analogous food: plain fermented milk / kefir (GI ~36, University of Sydney GI Database; Ostman et al., 2001, Am J Clin Nutr). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (36) × carbohydrate per serving (16.66 g per 200 g serving) / 100 = 6.0. The added sugar (8.33 g/100 g) may modestly elevate the effective GI of the packaged sweetened variant compared to plain kefir; the estimate is therefore conservative.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage classification (8.33 g sugar/100 g, zero dietary fibre); low sodium (50 mg/100 g) is a positive factor but does not offset SSB risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this sweetened packaged kefir and choose plain, unsweetened kefir instead, as added sugar in beverages independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, despite the product's low sodium content of 50 mg/100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 8.33 g sugar/100 g and zero dietary fibre; liquid sugar raises postprandial glucose rapidly
type2_diabetes.guidance: This packaged kefir is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes because its 8.33 g/100 g sugar content with zero dietary fibre classifies it as a sugar-sweetened beverage, causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation; plain unsweetened kefir (sugar ~0 g/100 g) is a suitable alternative that retains probiotic benefits without glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar (8.33 g/100 g) in a beverage raises CVD risk; saturated fat (~2.1 g/100 g) is moderate; live fermented cultures may confer modest cardioprotective benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this sweetened packaged kefir due to its added sugar content (8.33 g/100 g), which is associated with elevated triglycerides and CVD risk, and should opt for plain unsweetened kefir to benefit from probiotic cultures without the adverse metabolic effects of liquid sugar.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Live fermented cultures modulate gut microbiome and immune response; sugar (8.33 g/100 g) below the 15 g/100 g RA hard gate; saturated fat (~2.1 g/100 g) below 5 g/100 g threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Packaged kefir is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as its live probiotic cultures support gut-immune modulation and its sugar level (8.33 g/100 g) does not reach the 15 g/100 g inflammatory threshold, though plain unsweetened kefir is preferred to minimise any inflammatory contribution from added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Packaged kefir is a ready-to-drink fermented dairy beverage produced by inoculating whole or low-fat milk with kefir grains — a symbiotic culture of lactic acid bacteria and yeasts — resulting in a mildly tangy, slightly effervescent, creamy drink. The fermentation process produces lactic acid, carbon dioxide, and small amounts of ethanol, giving kefir its characteristic sour flavour and thin yogurt-like consistency. Commercially packaged kefir sold in Singapore and globally typically contains added sugar (approximately 8.33 g/100 g), distinguishing it from plain unsweetened kefir. Per 100 g, this product provides 83 kcal, 5.83 g protein, 3.33 g fat, and 8.33 g carbohydrate (all as sugar), with 50 mg sodium and zero dietary fibre. The live probiotic cultures in kefir — including Lactobacillus kefiranofaciens, Lactococcus lactis, and various yeasts — support gut microbiome diversity and immune modulation, making kefir a functional food with documented benefits for digestive health and inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. However, the added sugar content classifies this packaged variant as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), triggering clinical caution for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes and hypertension. The glycemic index of kefir is estimated at approximately 36 (low GI), though the sweetened formulation may modestly elevate postprandial glucose compared to plain kefir. Kefir originated in the Caucasus region and is now widely consumed across Europe, Central Asia, and increasingly in Southeast Asia as a probiotic health drink. It is suitable for vegetarians, halal-certified in most commercial formulations, and gluten-free, but is not suitable for vegans or those with dairy allergies.
common_user_queries: Can I drink kefir if I have high blood pressure?
How much sugar is in kefir?
What is the difference between kefir and yogurt?
Is kefir suitable for lactose intolerance?
Is kefir good for gut health?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from Singapore HPB Foodo database (cr_id D02020005, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Secondary micronutrients cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170904) for kefir. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for fermented milk / kefir. Ontology codes verified against FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. Calcium reported as 0 mg/100 g and iron as 0 mg/100 g in the HPB source (cr_id D02020005). This is atypical for kefir — USDA FoodData Central reports approximately 150 mg calcium/100 g and 0.05 mg iron/100 g for comparable kefir. The HPB values are preserved as primary per source-priority rules. Users should be aware that the calcium and iron figures may reflect a specific commercial formulation rather than typical kefir composition. These values are confirmed zero per the HPB source and are not set to -1 as the source explicitly reports them. 2. Dietary fibre is 0.0 g/100 g — confirmed zero per HPB source; kefir is a liquid dairy product and naturally contains negligible dietary fibre. 3. Saturated fat (2.1 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (0.85 g/100 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.12 g/100 g), and all micronutrient values not reported in the HPB source are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 170904 (kefir, lowfat) as the closest approved global equivalent. 4. Trans fat is 0.0 g/100 g — confirmed negligible for fermented dairy products per USDA SR Legacy. 5. GI of 36 is an analogous estimate based on plain fermented milk / kefir from the University of Sydney GI Database; the sweetened packaged variant may have a modestly higher effective GI. 6. Potassium is 164 mg/100 g per USDA SR Legacy adaptation — below the 200 mg/100 g threshold referenced in the input health_suitability hypertension note, consistent with the SSB classification applied. 7. The input health_suitability entries for diabetes and hypertension have been preserved and mapped to type2_diabetes and hypertension health_context entries respectively. The rheumatoid_arthritis entry has been preserved in additional_conditions. A cardiovascular_disease entry has been added as required by the Veritas scoring model.
